================================================================
        BHARAT PROCESS INDUSTRIES PVT. LTD.
              INCIDENT REPORT FORM
================================================================
Report No      : IR-2024-007
Date of Report : 06-March-2024
Report Prepared By : Rajan Kumar (Technician Grade-II)
Reviewed By    : Vikram Reddy (Maintenance Supervisor)
Approved By    : Anil Gupta (Plant Manager)
----------------------------------------------------------------
SECTION 1 — INCIDENT DETAILS
----------------------------------------------------------------
Incident Date  : 05-March-2024
Incident Time  : 14:35 hrs (IST)
Location       : Building C, Ground Floor, Unit 3
Equipment      : Cooling Water Pump 104 (Tag: P-104)
Incident Type  : Equipment Failure — Bearing Failure (Unplanned Shutdown)
Severity       : HIGH — Production impact for 4.5 hours
SECTION 2 — WHAT HAPPENED
----------------------------------------------------------------
At 14:35 hrs on 05-March-2024, the DCS (Distributed Control System)
triggered a high bearing temperature alarm on Pump P-104. The alarm
setpoint is 75°C. Operator on duty (Mr. Prakash Tiwari) acknowledged
the alarm and initiated field check.
By 14:42 hrs, field technician confirmed bearing temperature at
78°C and rising. Pump was manually tripped at 14:45 hrs per SOP-CW-003
(Section 4.2 — Emergency Shutdown Procedure).
Post-shutdown inspection at 15:10 hrs revealed Drive End (DE) bearing
had failed. Physical inspection showed:
  - Bearing cage fragmented (SKF 6309)
  - Significant heat discolouration on bearing housing (blue/brown marks)
  - Grease carbonised — indicating overheating prior to failure
  - No evidence of water ingress or external contamination
Standby pump (P-105) was started at 15:20 hrs after system checks.
Normal cooling water flow restored to Unit 3 at 15:35 hrs.
Total production disruption: 4 hours 55 minutes.
SECTION 3 — ROOT CAUSE ANALYSIS
----------------------------------------------------------------
Immediate Cause:
  Drive End bearing (SKF 6309) failed due to thermal overload caused
  by inadequate lubrication.
Contributing Factor 1 — LUBRICATION INTERVAL BREACH:
  Previous lubrication record (WO-2024-001, dated 10-January-2024)
  shows last greasing was 54 days before this failure.
  OEM recommended interval (OEM-KBL-KDS450-001, Section 6.3) specifies
  maximum 45 days between lubrication cycles for this pump class.
  The PM schedule had NOT been updated to reflect OEM requirement —
  it was set at 90 days (quarterly).
Contributing Factor 2 — ELEVATED TEMPERATURE TREND IGNORED:
  WO-2024-001 (10-Jan-2024) already noted bearing temperature at 72°C —
  close to the 75°C alarm limit. No follow-up action was scheduled and
  no alert was raised to the maintenance supervisor at that time.
Contributing Factor 3 — NO CONDITION MONITORING:
  Pump P-104 has no online vibration monitoring. Vibration was last
  manually checked on 10-January-2024. If continuous monitoring was in

place, bearing degradation would have been detectable 7-10 days
  before failure via vibration signature changes.
Root Cause (Systemic):
  PM schedule for Pump P-104 (and P-105) was not aligned with OEM
  specifications. No escalation mechanism existed for near-limit
  temperature readings noted in maintenance records.
SECTION 4 — IMMEDIATE CORRECTIVE ACTIONS TAKEN
----------------------------------------------------------------
1. Bearing replaced with SKF 6309 (same spec — OEM approved)
2. Bearing housing cleaned and inspected — no damage to shaft or housing
3. Fresh grease applied (SKF LGEP 2 — OEM specified grade)
4. Pump recommissioned and performance verified
5. Pump run for 2 hours under observation before handing over to operations
SECTION 5 — PREVENTIVE ACTIONS (Long Term)
----------------------------------------------------------------
Action 1 : Revise PM schedule for P-104 and P-105 lubrication from
           90 days to 30 days. (Owner: Suresh Nair | Due: 10-Mar-2024)
Action 2 : Add rule in DCS — any bearing temperature reading above 70°C
           to trigger supervisor notification (not just operator alarm).
           (Owner: Instrumentation Dept - Rajesh Pillai | Due: 31-Mar-2024)
Action 3 : Conduct audit of ALL rotating equipment PM schedules vs OEM
           manuals to identify similar gaps. (Owner: Vikram Reddy | Due: 30-
Apr-2024)
Action 4 : Evaluate feasibility of online vibration monitoring for
           P-104, P-105, and BL-07 blower. (Owner: Anil Gupta | Due: 31-May-
2024)
Action 5 : Add "near-limit temperature readings" to the list of items
           requiring supervisor sign-off in maintenance work orders.
           (Owner: Anil Gupta | Due: 15-Mar-2024)
SECTION 6 — REGULATORY COMPLIANCE NOTE
----------------------------------------------------------------
This incident has been reviewed against OISD-STD-137 (Inspection of
Rotating Equipment). The failure is classified as a maintenance-related
incident, not a process safety incident. No reportable event under
Factory Act 1948 (Section 88A) as no injury or fire occurred.
Incident to be recorded in plant incident log as required under
Factory Act Rule 103 (Maharashtra Factory Rules).
SECTION 7 — SIMILAR INCIDENTS — HISTORICAL CHECK
----------------------------------------------------------------
A search of incident records was conducted:
- IR-2022-014 (Sep 2022): P-105 mechanical seal failure — different
  root cause (dry running). Not directly related.
- IR-2021-003 (Feb 2021): P-104 bearing failure — SAME pump, same bearing.
  Review of IR-2021-003 shows same contributing factor: lubrication interval
  was 67 days at time of that failure. CORRECTIVE ACTION FROM IR-2021-003
  WAS NOT SUSTAINED — PM schedule was apparently reset to 90 days at
  some point after 2021 without documented justification.
NOTE: The recurrence of same failure on same equipment is of concern.
This must be escalated to the Engineering Manager for review.
================================================================
Signatures:
Prepared by : Rajan Kumar        Date: 06-Mar-2024
Reviewed by : Vikram Reddy       Date: 07-Mar-2024
Approved by : Anil Gupta         Date: 08-Mar-2024
================================================================

================================================================
        STATUTORY INSPECTION REPORT
        (Under Factories Act 1948 & PESO Regulations)
================================================================
Inspection Agency  : Maharashtra Industrial Safety and Health (MISH)
Inspector Name     : Mr. P.V. Kulkarni (Inspector of Factories)
Inspector ID       : MISH-INS-0442
Facility           : Bharat Process Industries Pvt. Ltd.
Facility Address   : Plot 44, MIDC Taloja, Navi Mumbai - 410208
Date of Inspection : 15-January-2024
Report Date        : 22-January-2024
Report Reference   : MISH-IR-2024-BPIL-007
----------------------------------------------------------------

SECTION A — FACILITY INFORMATION
----------------------------------------------------------------
Factory License No  : FACT-MH-2016-00892
Factory Manager     : Mr. Anil Gupta
Contact Person      : Mr. Vikram Reddy (Sr. Maintenance Manager)
Units Inspected     : Unit 3 — Cooling Water and Steam System
Number of Workers   : 124 (total plant), 18 (Unit 3)
Previous Inspection : 14-July-2023 (Report Ref: MISH-IR-2023-BPIL-019)

SECTION B — EQUIPMENT INSPECTED
----------------------------------------------------------------

B.1  PRESSURE VESSEL — V-22 (Steam Pressure Vessel 22)
     Manufacturer         : BHEL
     Design Pressure      : 25.0 bar (g)
     Working Pressure     : 22.0 bar (g)
     Design Temperature   : 220°C
     Year of Manufacture  : 2017
     Capacity             : 8,000 litres
     Last Certificate No  : IBR-PV-2023-MH-00231

     Inspection Findings:
     (i)   External visual inspection: No corrosion, cracks, or
           deformations observed on shell, heads, or nozzles.
     (ii)  Thickness gauging (ultrasonic): Minimum recorded thickness
           18.2 mm at 12 measurement points. Design thickness: 18.5 mm.
           Corrosion allowance remaining: 2.7 mm. Adequate.
     (iii) Safety Relief Valve (SRV-22A): Set pressure verified at
           24.8 bar — within acceptable tolerance of ±2% of design.
           Lifting tested — satisfactory. Last calibration certificate
           valid up to December 2024.
     (iv)  Pressure gauge calibration: PG-V22 found reading 0.4 bar
           HIGH — calibration required. Non-compliance noted.
     (v)   Nameplate: Legible and intact.
     (vi)  Vessel log book: Maintained. All entries current.
     (vii) Foundation bolts: Inspected — no looseness observed.

     Compliance Status : CONDITIONAL PASS
     Non-Compliance    : Pressure gauge PG-V22 requires recalibration
                         within 30 days. Compliance report to be
                         submitted to MISH by 15-February-2024.
     Certificate Valid : 15-January-2024 to 14-July-2024
     Certificate No    : IBR-PV-2024-MH-00089

B.2  PRESSURE VESSEL — V-23 (Steam Pressure Vessel 23)
     Manufacturer         : BHEL
     Design Pressure      : 25.0 bar (g)
     Working Pressure     : 22.0 bar (g)
     Design Temperature   : 220°C
     Year of Manufacture  : 2017

Capacity             : 8,000 litres
     Last Certificate No  : IBR-PV-2023-MH-00232

     Inspection Findings:
     (i)   External visual inspection: Minor surface rust on saddle
           supports — wire brushing and painting recommended.
     (ii)  Thickness gauging: Minimum 18.6 mm — above design minimum.
     (iii) Safety Relief Valve (SRV-23A): Note — spring fatigue
           detected during this inspection. SRV replaced before
           certificate issue. New spring installed and set pressure
           calibrated at 24.5 bar. New calibration cert issued.
     (iv)  Pressure gauge PG-V23: Within calibration. Valid to Sep-2024.
     (v)   Manhole cover: Gasket replaced in February 2024 per plant
           maintenance records (WO-2024-006). Satisfactory.

     Compliance Status : PASS (with recommendations)
     Recommendation    : Repaint saddle supports within 3 months.
     Certificate Valid : 01-February-2024 to 31-July-2024
     Certificate No    : IBR-PV-2024-MH-00090

B.3  BOILER — BL-07 (Fire Tube Boiler)
     Manufacturer         : Thermax Ltd.
     Design Pressure      : 32.0 bar (g)
     Working Pressure     : 28.0 bar (g)
     Design Temperature   : 250°C
     Year of Manufacture  : 2016
     Capacity             : 1,000 kg/hr steam output
     Last IBR Certificate : IBR-BLR-2023-MH-00156
     Registration No      : IBR-BLR-REG-MH-2016-00892-BL07

     Inspection Findings:
     (i)   Fireside inspection: Soot deposits moderate — cleaning done
           before inspection. Refractory — minor crack on left wall
           repaired by plant team. Post-repair satisfactory.
     (ii)  Waterside inspection: Scale deposits within acceptable
           limits (< 1.5 mm). Water treatment records reviewed —
           chemical dosing records for Jan-2024 reviewed and adequate.
     (iii) Safety valves: Both SV-BL07-A and SV-BL07-B tested and
           set pressures verified. Satisfactory.
     (iv)  Gauge glass: Replaced per WO-2024-010. Discolouration on
           old gauge glass was noted as observed finding. New glass
           installed — satisfactory.
     (v)   Burner and fuel system: Gas valve train inspected.
           All solenoid valves tested. Flame failure relay tested.
     (vi)  Water level controls: Low water cut-off tested — tripped
           correctly at set level. High water alarm tested — OK.
     (vii) Blowdown valve: Leaking slightly past seat. Maintenance
           notified — corrective action required within 15 days.

     Compliance Status : CONDITIONAL PASS
     Non-Compliance 1  : Blowdown valve (BDV-BL07) seat leakage —
                         repair required within 15 days.
     Non-Compliance 2  : IBR-registered operator log book incomplete
                         for 3 entries in December 2023.
                         Complete records within 7 days.
     IBR Certificate   : 18-March-2024 to 17-March-2025
     Certificate No    : IBR-BLR-2024-MH-00103

SECTION C — GENERAL FACTORY ACT COMPLIANCE OBSERVATIONS
----------------------------------------------------------------
C.1  First Aid facilities: Adequate. First aid boxes inspected —
     stocked as per Schedule 18 (Factory Act).
C.2  Fire extinguishers near Unit 3: 4 CO2 and 2 DCP units present.
     All within service dates. PASS.

C.3  Emergency exits: 3 exits available for Unit 3. Clearly marked.
     Exit signs illuminated. PASS.
C.4  Personal Protective Equipment: PPE board present. Helmets and
     safety shoes observed in use. PASS.
C.5  Hazardous Chemical Storage (TK-09 area): Chemical (biocide and
     scale inhibitor) drums stored correctly. MSDS displayed. PASS.

SECTION D — SUMMARY OF NON-COMPLIANCES AND DEADLINES
----------------------------------------------------------------
No. | Equipment | Non-Compliance                  | Deadline
--- | --------- | ------------------------------- | -----------
1   | V-22      | Recalibrate pressure gauge PG-V22 | 15-Feb-2024
2   | BL-07     | Repair blowdown valve BDV-BL07    | 01-Apr-2024
3   | BL-07     | Complete IBR operator log entries | 25-Jan-2024

SECTION E — NEXT INSPECTION
----------------------------------------------------------------
Next statutory inspection scheduled: July 2024
Bharat Process Industries is required to submit compliance
certificates for items in Section D by the deadlines stated.
Failure to comply will result in show cause notice under
Section 36A of the Factories Act, 1948.

================================================================
Signed:
Inspector         : P.V. Kulkarni, MISH
Plant Manager     : Anil Gupta, BPIL
Date              : 22-January-2024
================================================================

Within acceptable limits. Fouling rate higher than expected - chemical dosing may be insufficient. Closed 2000 Escalate to process team for water treatment review

rows: Vibration within limits. Bearing temperature slightly elevated at 72°C vs limit of 75°C. Lubrication completed.
Annual PESO inspection - thickness gauging and safety valve test
Wall thickness 18.2mm vs design 18.5mm. Safety valve set pressure verified at 24.8 bar. Certificate issued.
Biannual tube bundle inspection and cleaning
3 tubes found with minor pitting. Plugged. Overall efficiency estimated at 94%. Chemical cleaning completed.
Pump tripped on high vibration alarm - investigation and repair
Mechanical seal - Flowserve FS-28, Impeller wear ring

Mechanical seal failed causing internal leakage. Impeller wear ring worn beyond tolerance (2.8mm vs limit 2.5mm). Both replaced. Root cause: seal ran dry for approx 15 minutes during valve maintenance on 01-Feb.
Check cooling water supply logic before any valve isolation
Tube-side fouling confirmed. Hydrojet cleaning performed. DP reduced from 3.2 bar to 1.1 bar post cleaning.
Biannual inspection and safety relief valve check
Relief valve spring fatigue detected. Replaced. Set pressure recalibrated to 24.5 bar. Vessel shell inspection clear.
Monthly filter change and belt tension check
Belt tension slightly low on drive side. Adjusted. Filters heavily loaded - consider reducing change interval to 3 weeks

DE bearing failed. Temperature reached 81°C before shutdown. Bearing replaced. Suspected cause: inadequate lubrication interval. Last greasing was WO-2024-001 dated 10-Jan (54 days gap vs recommended 45 days).
Reduce lubrication interval to 30 days. Review PM schedule.
Monthly drift eliminator check and basin cleaning
Basin sludge level high. Fill media section 4B damaged and replaced. Water distribution header nozzles 3 found blocked - cleared.
IBR certificate renewed. Gauge glass replaced (discoloration observed). Refractory lining minor crack repaired. All safety interlocks tested and certified.
Valve not achieving full open position
Actuator limit switch misaligned due to vibration. Realigned and locked. Valve stroke tested - 0 to 100% in 12 seconds.
Add lock wire to limit switch
Monthly lubrication - revised schedule per WO-2024-008
Lubrication completed. Bearing temperature 63°C - normal. Vibration 2.1 mm/s - within limit of 4.5 mm/s.
Quarterly tank inspection and level calibration
Float switch corroded and replaced. Tank lining in good condition. Chemical inventory reconciled.
Post-repair commissioning and performance test after WO-2024-004
Flow rate achieved: 420 m3/hr vs design 450 m3/hr (93.3%). Vibration 2.8 mm/s - acceptable. Temperature rise normal. Minor performance gap - monitor over 2 weeks.
Follow up DP check post WO-2024-005 cleaning
DP now 1.4 bar - slight increase from 1.1 bar post cleaning. Within acceptable limits. Fouling rate higher than expected - chemical dosing may be insufficient.
Escalate to process team for water treatment review

pages.text:
================================================================
    BHARAT PROCESS INDUSTRIES PVT. LTD.
    NEAR MISS & OBSERVATION REPORTS — UNIT 3
    Compiled: 2022 and 2023 Records
================================================================

NEAR MISS REPORT NM-2022-008
Date        : 14-July-2022
Location    : Building C, Ground Floor
Equipment   : Pump P-104
Reported By : Prakash Tiwari (Operator)
Supervisor  : Suresh Nair

Description:
During monsoon season, excessive humidity in Building C caused
condensation on the motor terminal box of Pump P-104. The
terminal box cover was found partially open during morning
rounds. No electrical fault occurred but the potential for
short circuit was high. Cover secured and sealed with silicone.

Root Cause: Terminal box cover latch was corroded and not
latching properly. Replaced latch.

Corrective Action: Inspect all motor terminal boxes in Unit 3
for latch condition before every monsoon season (June).

Closed: Yes | Action Verified: Yes | Date: 20-July-2022

----------------------------------------------------------------

NEAR MISS REPORT NM-2022-014
Date        : 03-September-2022
Location    : Building C, Ground Floor
Equipment   : Pump P-105
Reported By : Sanjay Patil (Operator)
Supervisor  : Vikram Reddy

Description:
During routine changeover from P-104 to P-105, P-105 started
but immediately tripped on high current (58A vs limit 52A).
Investigation found discharge valve was opened too quickly
(within 5 seconds vs procedure requirement of 30-60 seconds
per SOP-CW-003 Section 4.2 Step 3). No damage occurred.

Root Cause: Operator error — new operator (Sanjay Patil, 3 months
experience) not fully familiar with SOP requirement on slow
valve opening.

Corrective Action: Refresher training for all operators on
SOP-CW-003. Valve opening time requirement to be added to
DCS operator guidance screen.

Closed: Yes | Action Verified: Partial | Date: 10-Sep-2022
NOTE: DCS guidance screen update still pending as of Jan 2024.

----------------------------------------------------------------

INCIDENT REPORT IR-2022-019
Date        : 22-September-2022
Location    : Building D, Level 1
Equipment   : V-22 (Steam Pressure Vessel 22)
Reported By : Amit Sharma (Technician)
Supervisor  : Vikram Reddy

Description:
Safety relief valve SRV-22A lifted spontaneously during normal
operation at working pressure of 21.8 bar. Set pressure was
24.8 bar — lifting at 21.8 bar indicates valve set pressure
had drifted significantly. Steam release lasted 4 minutes.
No injury. Area evacuated per EEP-001.

Root Cause: SRV spring fatigue. Valve had been in service for
5 years without replacement. OEM (Spirax Sarco) recommends
spring replacement every 3 years for this service.

Corrective Action:
1. SRV-22A spring replaced and recalibrated.
2. All SRVs in plant to be audited for last spring replacement date.
3. SRV spring replacement added to PM schedule (3-year interval).

Closed: Yes | Action Verified: Yes | Date: 30-Sep-2022

----------------------------------------------------------------

NEAR MISS REPORT NM-2023-003
Date        : 18-July-2023
Location    : Building C, Ground Floor
Equipment   : Pump P-104
Reported By : Ramesh Yadav (Operator)
Supervisor  : Suresh Nair

Description:
Second monsoon season observation (see also NM-2022-008):
Despite latch repair in July 2022, the terminal box cover of
P-104 motor was again found loosely closed. Investigation
found the silicone sealant from 2022 had degraded. Additionally,
the motor ventilation louvers had insect nesting debris
blocking 30% of airflow, contributing to higher motor temperature
(motor casing 72°C vs normal 55-60°C).

NOTE: This is the SAME equipment, SAME location, SAME season
(monsoon July) as NM-2022-008. Pattern is emerging.

Root Cause: Seasonal — monsoon humidity causes seal degradation.
Insect nesting in ventilation is a recurring monsoon problem.

Corrective Action:
1. Terminal box resealed with better grade silicone (Dow Corning 732).
2. Motor ventilation inspected and cleaned.
3. Pre-monsoon inspection checklist (June each year) to be created
   specifically covering: terminal box seal, motor ventilation,
   any outdoor/semi-outdoor equipment.

Closed: Yes | Action Verified: Partial | Date: 25-July-2023
NOTE: Pre-monsoon checklist was drafted but not formally approved.

----------------------------------------------------------------

NEAR MISS REPORT NM-2023-011
Date        : 12-October-2023
Location    : Building C, Ground Floor
Equipment   : MV-31 (Motor Control Valve)
Reported By : Deepak Joshi (Technician)
Supervisor  : Suresh Nair

Description:
During periodic valve stroke test, MV-31 (cooling water minimum
flow bypass) was found stuck at 65% open — not achieving full

stroke. This valve is critical for pump protection. If pump
runs with discharge valve closed, MV-31 ensures minimum flow
to prevent overheating. A stuck valve could lead to pump
damage or mechanical seal failure.

Root Cause: Actuator limit switch had shifted due to vibration
from nearby pump (P-104). This is the same root cause as
WO-2024-011 (March 2024) where the same fault recurred.

Corrective Action (2023): Limit switch realigned.
NOTE: Corrective action from 2023 did NOT include lock-wiring
the limit switch — same fault recurred in March 2024 (see WO-2024-011).
Lock wire was only added in March 2024 repair.

Closed: Yes | Action Verified: Yes | Date: 18-Oct-2023

----------------------------------------------------------------

NEAR MISS REPORT NM-2023-018
Date        : 28-November-2023
Location    : Building C, Level 2
Equipment   : HX-11 (Heat Exchanger 11)
Reported By : Deepak Joshi (Technician)
Supervisor  : Vikram Reddy

Description:
During inspection of HX-11, technician noticed a small
pinhole leak on the tube-side nozzle flange. Cooling water
was dripping at approximately 20 ml/min. Leak was in an
area that could contact hot surfaces on adjacent equipment.

No fire or injury. Pump was isolated and flange gasket replaced
as emergency corrective action.

Root Cause: Gasket degradation — original gasket was spiralwound
CS/graphite. For cooling water service with chemical dosing,
Thermax (OEM) recommends PTFE envelope gasket. Wrong gasket
material had been installed during previous maintenance
(unknown date — no record found).

Corrective Action:
1. Replaced with correct PTFE envelope gasket (OEM recommended).
2. Check material specification on all HX flanges during
   next scheduled maintenance.

Closed: Yes | Action Verified: Yes | Date: 05-Dec-2023

================================================================
PATTERN ANALYSIS NOTE (Added by Vikram Reddy, 20-April-2024)
================================================================
On reviewing the above records for the IR-2024-007 investigation,
the following patterns are visible:

PATTERN 1 — MONSOON VULNERABILITY (July-September):
  NM-2022-008 (Jul-2022), NM-2023-003 (Jul-2023) — same equipment
  (P-104), same season, same basic cause (moisture ingress/motor
  issues). Pre-monsoon inspection checklist was drafted but never
  formally approved. Risk of NM-2024 in Jul-2024 is HIGH if
  the checklist is not activated.
  ACTION: Approve and implement pre-monsoon checklist before June 2024.

PATTERN 2 — CORRECTIVE ACTIONS NOT SUSTAINED:
  NM-2022-014 → DCS screen update never completed (Jan 2024 note)
  NM-2023-011 → Lock wire not added in 2023 → same fault March 2024

IR-2021-003 → Lubrication interval corrected but later reset to
                wrong value → same bearing failure March 2024 (IR-2024-007)
  SYSTEMIC ISSUE: No verification system exists to confirm long-term
  corrective action effectiveness. Actions are "closed" too quickly
  without follow-up verification after 3-6 months.

PATTERN 3 — PRE-ALARM TREND IGNORED:
  WO-2024-001 (Jan 2024) noted bearing temp at 72°C on P-104.
  Operator Round Sheets show temp rising from 2-March onwards.
  No supervisor review triggered until alarm at 75°C on 2-March.
  RCA on IR-2024-007 identified same pattern.
  RECOMMENDATION: Implement trend-based alerting, not just
  threshold-based alarming.

================================================================
END OF DOCUMENT
================================================================

================================================================
    KIRLOSKAR BROTHERS LIMITED
    OPERATION & MAINTENANCE MANUAL
    Model: KDS-450 Centrifugal Pump Series
    Document Ref: OEM-KBL-KDS450-001
    Issue: Rev 3.1 | Date: March 2020
================================================================
(EXCERPT — Sections 6, 7, and 9 relevant to AssetIQ demo)
================================================================

SECTION 6 — LUBRICATION
----------------------------------------------------------------

6.1  BEARING LUBRICATION TYPE
The KDS-450 series uses grease-lubricated antifriction (rolling
element) bearings. Approved grease type: Lithium complex grease,
NLGI Grade 2. Recommended grade: SKF LGEP 2 or equivalent.

DO NOT mix different grease types. Mixing can cause thickener
incompatibility and accelerated bearing failure.

6.2  GREASE QUANTITY
Drive End (DE) Bearing   : 35 grams per lubrication cycle
Non-Drive End (NDE) Bearing: 25 grams per lubrication cycle

Over-greasing is as harmful as under-greasing. Excess grease
causes churning, heat generation, and premature bearing failure.
Use a calibrated grease gun to ensure correct quantity.

6.3  LUBRICATION INTERVAL
Lubrication frequency depends on operating conditions:

  Condition A — Standard operation (<40°C ambient, <3000 hrs/year):
                Lubrication every 45 days

  Condition B — Elevated temperature (40-50°C ambient) or
                continuous operation (>3000 hrs/year):
                Lubrication every 30 days

  Condition C — High temperature (>50°C ambient) or dusty/humid
                environment:
                Lubrication every 15 days

IMPORTANT: For pumps operating in Indian coastal or monsoon
environments, Condition B or C applies. Consult Kirloskar
regional service team if in doubt.

6.4  BEARING TEMPERATURE LIMITS

  Normal Operating Range  : 40°C to 65°C above ambient
  Advisory Limit          : 70°C (investigate cause if sustained)
  Warning Alarm           : 75°C (reduce load or inspect)
  Maximum Trip Limit      : 85°C (immediate shutdown mandatory)

  NOTE: A sudden rise in bearing temperature of >10°C without
  change in operating conditions is a strong indicator of
  lubrication failure or bearing damage. Stop pump and investigate.

SECTION 7 — MAINTENANCE SCHEDULE
----------------------------------------------------------------

7.1  DAILY CHECKS (Operator)
     [ ] Check suction and discharge pressure gauges
     [ ] Check bearing temperature (if thermometer fitted)

[ ] Listen for abnormal noise (grinding, knocking)
     [ ] Check gland/seal leakage (max 30 drops/minute for
         gland packing; zero for mechanical seal except quench flow)
     [ ] Check motor current from MCC

7.2  WEEKLY CHECKS (Technician)
     [ ] Manual vibration check with vibration meter
         Acceptable limit: 2.8 mm/s (ISO 10816 Zone A)
         Action required: >4.5 mm/s (ISO 10816 Zone C)
     [ ] Check coupling condition (if coupling guard allows)
     [ ] Check baseplate for any cracks or loose foundation bolts

7.3  MONTHLY (Technician + Supervisor sign-off)
     [ ] Complete lubrication (Section 6.1 to 6.3)
     [ ] Check seal quench flow and quench water quality
     [ ] Measure and record vibration (both DE and NDE)
     [ ] Record bearing temperatures under load
     [ ] Check all instrumentation — pressure, temperature, flow

7.4  QUARTERLY (Maintenance Engineer)
     [ ] Check impeller clearance — max wear ring clearance 2.5 mm.
         Replace wear ring if clearance exceeds this limit.
     [ ] Check shaft runout — max 0.05 mm TIR
     [ ] Coupling alignment check — max 0.10 mm offset, 0.05 mm angular
     [ ] Check all gaskets and O-rings for condition

7.5  ANNUAL (OEM Service recommended)
     [ ] Complete pump overhaul — disassembly, inspection, reassembly
     [ ] Replace all bearings (regardless of condition) if >8000 hours run
     [ ] Replace mechanical seal if >12 months continuous operation
     [ ] Hydraulic performance test — compare with factory test curve

SECTION 9 — TROUBLESHOOTING
----------------------------------------------------------------

Problem 1: HIGH BEARING TEMPERATURE
  Possible Causes:
  a) Insufficient lubrication — Check interval vs Section 6.3
  b) Over-greasing — Verify quantity per Section 6.2
  c) Wrong grease type — Verify grease specification
  d) Bearing damage — Check vibration, listen for noise
  e) Misalignment — Check coupling alignment per 7.4
  f) Overloading — Check motor current vs nameplate

  Action: If temperature exceeds 75°C — STOP PUMP.
          Do not restart without investigation.

Problem 2: HIGH VIBRATION
  Possible Causes:
  a) Impeller damage or partial blockage
  b) Bearing failure
  c) Cavitation (suction pressure too low)
  d) Coupling imbalance or wear
  e) Resonance — check baseplate and piping supports

  Action: Vibration >6 mm/s — STOP PUMP IMMEDIATELY.

Problem 3: LOSS OF FLOW / LOW PRESSURE
  Possible Causes:
  a) Air entrainment in suction
  b) Worn impeller or wear rings (check clearance)
  c) Partially closed suction valve
  d) Pump rotating in wrong direction
  e) Speed too low (check motor frequency)

Problem 4: MECHANICAL SEAL LEAKAGE
  Possible Causes:
  a) Dry running — ensure quench water always flowing during
     pump operation
  b) Worn seal faces — replace seal
  c) Incorrect installation — check as per seal drawing

  WARNING: If seal leaks more than 60 ml/hour — STOP PUMP.
           Seal failure can lead to fire if product is flammable.

SECTION 10 — SPARE PARTS LIST (Recommended)
----------------------------------------------------------------
Part Description          | Part Number    | Qty Recommended
--------------------------|----------------|------------------
Drive End Bearing         | SKF 6309       | 2 per pump
Non-Drive End Bearing     | SKF 6307       | 2 per pump
Mechanical Seal (complete)| Flowserve FS-28| 1 per pump
Impeller Wear Ring (pair) | KBL-KDS450-WR  | 1 set per pump
Shaft Sleeve              | KBL-KDS450-SS  | 1 per pump
Casing Gasket Set         | KBL-KDS450-GS  | 2 sets per pump
Coupling Insert           | KBL-KDS450-CI  | 2 per pump

================================================================
For technical support: Kirloskar Brothers Ltd.
Regional Office — Mumbai: +91-22-4019-8800
Email: service.west@kirloskarbros.com
================================================================
END OF EXCERPT
================================================================

rows: Slight increase in bearing temp noted - within limits
Bearing temp 70°C - reported to shift supervisor verbally
Bearing temp 72°C - approaching warning. Will monitor closely.
Rajan Kumar visited and checked - said will monitor
ALARM TRIGGERED - 75°C. Shift supervisor Suresh Nair informed.
Running on alarm. P-105 being prepared for start.
Temp 78°C. Per SOP-CW-003 Section 4.5 - initiating emergency stop
TRIPPED - Emergency stop per SOP. Breakdown WO raised.
P-104 recommissioned after bearing replacement (WO-2024-008). Running OK.
Normal - bearing temp stable post repair
Normal. Monthly lubrication due in 14 days per new schedule.
